narrative: As an Asian male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40—above the typical 3.21 average—and a strong LSAT score of 38, which beats the dataset average of 36.70, you've built an impressive academic foundation that many students aspire to. Your family falls into the middle income bracket, and you're planning to attend full-time, which is the norm for over 92% of students in this dataset. However, the admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 65% chance that you would fail the bar on your first attempt—a threshold that led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes candidates predicted to pass. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the average of 5.53, signaling to the model that your later academic performance placed you in the lower tier compared to peers who typically pass the bar. This stood out as the top driver, outweighing other strengths in the model's eyes. Next in importance, your LSAT score worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success by demonstrating the analytical skills associated with bar passers. Your undergraduate GPA also helped, nudging things positively as it exceeded the norm and correlates with strong legal outcomes for many. While these positives couldn't fully offset the Year 3 concern, they show the model recognized your potential in key areas. Compared to typical students who pass, your profile aligns well on LSAT and GPA but diverges on that later decile, which often flags risks in bar preparation. To move forward, consider retaking courses or building a stronger senior-year record to boost your decile ranking, as this could dramatically shift future predictions. You might also seek targeted bar prep resources early, given your other strengths, and reapply after addressing this gap—many students in similar situations succeed by focusing here. Your background as an Asian male applicant doesn't change the feature-driven logic, but it positions you among high-achieving peers who often overcome hurdles with persistence. Keep pushing; this is one data point, not your destiny.
